claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 21 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 13 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 21, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 5 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin your Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

The first thing I need to know is about the child (or children) you're claiming for.

**How old is the child you want to claim Child Benefit for?**

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children, let's start with one child first, and then we can check the others afterwards. Please also let me know the child's name so I can keep track.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit. I actually have three children - Alex who's 5, Blake who's 21, and Charlie who's 13.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing those details! Let me help you check eligibility for each of your three children: Alex (5), Blake (21), and Charlie (13).

I can already give you some initial information based on their ages:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Let me get the information about Blake separately:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Here's what I can tell you so far:

---

**Blake (21 years old):** Unfortunately, Blake is **not eligible** for Child Benefit. Children must be either:
- Under 16, OR
- Aged 16-19 and in approved education or training

Since Blake is 20 or over, they no longer qualify for Child Benefit.

---

**Alex (5) and Charlie (13):** Both are under 16, which is great news! Children under 16 automatically meet the age requirement. However, I need to ask a few more questions to complete the eligibility check.

Let me continue with the next question for Alex and Charlie:

**Are both Alex and Charlie working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

(Given their ages of 5 and 13, I'd expect the answer is yes, but I need to confirm to follow the assessment properly.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Oh, I see about Blake - that's a shame, but it makes sense.

For Alex and Charlie - no, they're not working at all. Alex is only 5, still in primary school, and Charlie is 13, so they're both just at school full-time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That makes perfect sense! Let me continue the assessment for Alex and Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good, that's confirmed. Next question:

**Are Alex and Charlie NOT receiving any benefits in their own right?**

This means benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit claimed by the child themselves (not benefits you receive for them). Given their ages, this is unlikely, but I need to check.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receive any benefits in their own names. They're just kids - I'm the one who handles all the family finances and any benefits would be in my name, not theirs.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's confirmed. Next question:

**Are Alex and Charlie in any kind of apprenticeship?**

Different apprenticeships have different rules:
- **Disqualifying apprenticeships** (would make them ineligible): All apprenticeships in England, Modern Apprenticeships in Scotland, and most apprenticeships in Wales
- **Approved apprenticeships** (still eligible): Foundation Apprenticeships in Wales

Given their ages (5 and 13), I expect they're not in any apprenticeship, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not. Alex is only 5 and Charlie is 13 - they're both far too young for apprenticeships. They're just in regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check some questions about you (the claimant) rather than the children.

**Do you meet UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **ANY** of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. **You have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **You have pre-settled status** and meet certain conditions (e.g., working, seeking work, studying, or have sufficient resources)
4. **You're a Crown servant** living abroad

The most common scenario is simply that you normally live in the UK. Does this apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm in Manchester, so no issues there with residency.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Living in Manchester qualifies you for the residency requirement.

Now I need to check your responsibility for the children:

**Are you responsible for Alex and Charlie?**

You qualify if **ANY** of the following apply:

1. **You live with the children** – the most common situation
2. **You contribute to their upkeep** – paying at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **You pay maintenance** – under a court order or agreement

Do you live with Alex and Charlie, or contribute to their upkeep in one of these ways?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they both live at home with me. I'm their parent and they live with me full-time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Living with them full-time definitely counts as being responsible for the children.

Now an important question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming (or planning to claim) Child Benefit for Alex or Charlie?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- Another family member

Only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child at a time. If someone else is already claiming, you'd need to agree between yourselves who should receive it, or HMRC would need to make a decision.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming for them. It's just me - I'm their main carer and no one else has claimed Child Benefit for either of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Now I need to check about the children's living situation:

**Where do Alex and Charlie currently live?**

Are they:
- **Living normally at home** with you
- **In hospital** for an extended period
- **In local authority care**
- **In a fostering arrangement**

From what you've told me, it sounds like they're living normally at home with you - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex lives at home with me normally, yes. But Charlie - he's actually been in local authority care for a couple of months now, though he does come home to stay with me on the weekends.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying that. Since Alex and Charlie are in different situations, let me handle them separately.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is helpful information. Let me ask about Charlie's care situation:

**For Charlie - does he spend at least 24 hours a week at home with you?**

You mentioned he comes home on weekends. If he typically stays overnight for the whole weekend (e.g., Friday to Sunday), that would likely meet the 24-hour threshold.

There are special rules for children in care:
- Child Benefit normally continues for up to **8 weeks** after a child goes into care
- However, you can **still claim** if the child spends at least **24 hours a week** at home
- You can also claim if the child spends **2 consecutive nights per week** or **7+ days in a row** at home

Does Charlie spend at least 24 hours a week with you during his weekend visits?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he does. He comes home Friday after school and stays until Sunday evening, so that's definitely more than 24 hours a week with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news - since Charlie spends more than 24 hours a week at home with you, that meets the requirement. Now one more important question about Charlie:

**Is the local council paying towards Charlie's accommodation or maintenance while he's in care?**

This is asking whether the council is financially covering the costs of Charlie's placement (such as paying for his accommodation, food, and care while he's in the local authority care setting during the week).

If the council **is** paying for his care, this may affect your eligibility for Child Benefit for Charlie.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, the local authority is paying for his care placement during the week. They cover the costs of where he stays when he's in care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I understand. Unfortunately, this affects Charlie's eligibility. Let me now complete the assessment for Alex, who lives at home with you normally.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For Alex, who lives normally at home with you:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

I assume not, since Alex lives at home with you full-time, but I need to confirm to complete the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, not at all. Alex just lives at home with me - there's no council involvement or payments for Alex whatsoever.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I now have all the information I need to give you a complete assessment for all three children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Your Child Benefit Eligibility Summary

Here are the results for your three children:

### ✅ Alex (age 5) — **ELIGIBLE**
Alex meets all the requirements for Child Benefit:
- Under 16 years old
- Not working 24+ hours per week
- Not receiving benefits in their own right
- Not in an apprenticeship
- You live in the UK (Manchester)
- You are responsible for Alex (lives with you full-time)
- No one else is claiming for Alex
- No council involvement in Alex's care

### ❌ Blake (age 21) — **NOT ELIGIBLE**
Blake is over the maximum age limit. Child Benefit is only available for children who are:
- Under 16, OR
- Aged 16-19 and in approved education or training

At 21, Blake no longer qualifies.

### ❌ Charlie (age 13) — **NOT ELIGIBLE**
Although Charlie meets the age requirement and spends more than 24 hours per week at home with you (which would normally allow eligibility to continue), the local authority is paying towards Charlie's accommodation and maintenance during his care placement. This means you cannot receive Child Benefit for Charlie while the council is funding his care.

---

## Next Steps for Alex

To claim Child Benefit for Alex, you should:

1. **Apply online** or by post using **form CH2**
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** — if you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may have to pay back some or all of the benefit through tax

Payments are usually made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain other benefits).

👉 More information: [How to claim Child Benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

---

**Regarding Charlie:** If Charlie's circumstances change in the future (for example, if he returns home full-time and the council is no longer paying for his care), you may be able to claim Child Benefit for him at that point.

Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify?

reasonings: Alex (age 5) is ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. Alex meets all the requirements: (1) Under 16 years old, (2) Not working 24+ hours per week, (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Not in an apprenticeship, (5) You live in the UK, (6) You are responsible for Alex as they live with you, (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, (8) Alex lives normally at home, and (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 21) is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. Children must be either under 16, or aged 16-19 and in approved education or training. At 21 years old, Blake exceeds the maximum age limit and therefore does not qualify as a 'qualifying child' for Child Benefit purposes.

Charlie (age 13) is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. Although Charlie meets the age requirement (under 16), is not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, and not in an apprenticeship - and although Charlie spends more than 24 hours per week at home with you (which would normally allow eligibility to continue while in care) - the local authority is paying towards Charlie's accommodation and maintenance during his care placement. This means you cannot receive Child Benefit for Charlie while the council is funding his care.
arguments.overall_reasoning: Of the three children, only Alex (age 5) is eligible for Child Benefit. Blake (age 21) is too old to qualify as they exceed the maximum age limit of 20. Charlie (age 13), despite meeting most criteria and spending weekends at home, is ineligible because the local council is paying for their care placement during the week.

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible
